---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
#format: revealjs
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: false
incremental: false
header-includes:
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  \usepackage{tikz}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric}
  \usetikzlibrary{overlay-beamer-styles}
---

```{=latex}
% TITLE SLIDE
\title{{\fontsize{15pt}{16pt}\selectfont Applied Psychometrics}:\\ studying subclinical psychopathologies\\ \vspace{1.5em} \raggedright 
\emph{8.1 - Z-scores and non parametric percentiles}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}

\titlegraphic{

\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\begin{center}
\vspace{-2.2em}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
\end{center}
}
\date{2026}
\maketitle
```

```{r}
#initialize a quato template for resuse
knitr::opts_template$set(
  myplot = list(
    echo = FALSE,
    fig.show = "asis",
    dpi = 100,
    fig.width = 7,
    fig.height = 5
  )
)

```
## Z-score

The most common and widespread method to determine the percentiles is the **z-score** transformation


## Z-score


$$ \mathbf{Z} = \frac{(X - M)}{SD}$$

$\mathbf{Z}$ = Z score

$X$ = observed score

$M$ = Mean of the normative sample

$SD$ = Standard deviation of the normative sample.


## Z-score to percentiles

If the data distribution is normal, z-scores can be easily mapped to percentiles.


## Z-score to percentiles

![](Figures/gaussiana.png){fig-align="center" widt="80%"}


## A few "famous" z-scores

- $\mathbf{Z = -1.64}$ denotes the $5^{th}$ percentile.
\pause

- $\mathbf{Z = -2}$, $\mathbf{Z = -1.5}$ are commonly used because they are easy to remember (the correspond to $2.2^{th}$ and $6.7^{th}$ percentile, respectively)


## Interpreting z-scores
\centering
z-scores can be easily interpreted.

See the \href{https://app.hsancamillo.it/app/zscore_crawfordt1}{\underline{\textbf{online app!}}}

## Important!

If the data distribution is not normal, than z-scores could not correspond to the expected percentile!

```{r}
#| opts.label: myplot
#| fig.height: 4


par(mfrow=c(1,2), mar=c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))
set.seed(100)
x1=rnorm(100, mean=10)
x2=scale(-exp(rnorm(100, mean=20)))+10

hist(x1, xlab="score", main="normal (gaussian) distribution")
mtext("Z-score: ok", side = 3)
hist(x2, xlab="score", main="non-normal distribution")
mtext("Z-score: not ok", side = 3)


```


## Alternative: non-parametric percentile


Non parametric percentiles are distribution-free and are calculated by ranking data and calculating the empirical proportion of data below a specific value.


## Example of percentiles provided with a test 

\scriptsize

| Percentile | Score |
| ---------- | ----- |
| 5          | 12    |
| 10         | 14    |
| 15         | 15    |
| 20         | 16    |
| 25         | 17    |
| 30         | 18    |
| 35         | 19    |
| 40         | 20    |
| 45         | 21    |
| 50         | 22    |
| 55         | 23    |
| 60         | 24    |
| 65         | 25    |
| 70         | 26    |
| 75         | 27    |
| 80         | 28    |
| 85         | 29    |
| 90         | 30    |
